results.possibleIdentity: Walter Wright is a pioneering interdisciplinary artist, video animator, and software engineer active since the mid-1960s. Originally trained as an architect (B.Arch, University of Manitoba, 1965) and in systems design (M.A.Sc, University of Waterloo, 1967), he began creating electronic images and sound in 1966. In the early 1970s, he became one of the first video animators at Computer Image Corp, where he utilized the Scanimate analog computer system. He is notable for his collaboration with Ed Emshwiller on the Emmy-nominated computer animation 'Scapemates' (1971). Wright was deeply involved in the New York avant-garde scene, serving as an Associate Director at The Kitchen and succeeding Nam June Paik as artist-in-residence at the Experimental Television Center from 1973 to 1976. During this residency, he pioneered live video performance, touring with the Paik/Abe Video Synthesizer. His early career was significantly influenced by the work of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) group and the research conducted at Bell Laboratories. In his later career, he developed the 'Video Shredder' performance system and co-founded the 119 Gallery (originally 911 Gallery), one of the first digital art galleries on the web.
